neighborhoods.name: Harlem
neighborhoods.fact: Birthplace of the Harlem Renaissance in the 1920s. The Apollo Theater launched Ella Fitzgerald, James Brown & Stevie Wonder. It begins exactly where Central Park ends.

neighborhoods.name: Washington Heights
neighborhoods.fact: Manhattan's highest natural point at 265 ft. George Washington commanded his Revolutionary War forces from here. The A-train runs express through this stretch.

neighborhoods.name: Inwood
neighborhoods.fact: The last old-growth forest on Manhattan island survives here. Legend says the Dutch purchased all of Manhattan Island from the Lenape near this spot for just $24 in trade goods.

zones.name: Upper East & West Side
zones.street_range: 72nd to 110th Street · Central Park is the dividing line

neighborhoods.name: Upper East Side
neighborhoods.fact: "Museum Mile" — 9 world-class museums packed into one strip of 5th Avenue, including the Met & Guggenheim. Among the most expensive real estate in the world.

neighborhoods.name: Upper West Side
neighborhoods.fact: Home to Lincoln Center, the Museum of Natural History & legendary deli Zabar's. Where Seinfeld, Westside Story, and Rosemary's Baby were all set.

neighborhoods.name: Morningside Heights
neighborhoods.fact: Columbia University was founded in 1754 — older than the United States. Grant's Tomb is here, and yes — both Ulysses S. Grant AND his wife are actually buried inside.

zones.name: Midtown
zones.street_range: 34th to 72nd Street · Penn Station & Grand Central are your anchors

neighborhoods.name: Midtown
neighborhoods.fact: Times Square draws 50 million visitors a year — more than the Eiffel Tower. The Empire State Building was constructed in just 410 days and has 1,576 steps to the top.

neighborhoods.name: Hell's Kitchen
neighborhoods.fact: Named after a brutal 19th-century gang, its gentler alias is "Clinton Hill." Most of Broadway's performers and crew live here. Great cheap food along 9th Avenue.

neighborhoods.name: Murray Hill
neighborhoods.fact: Named after Scottish merchant Robert Murray. The block known as "Koreatown" on 32nd Street has one of the densest concentrations of Korean restaurants outside of Seoul.

zones.name: Downtown
zones.street_range: 14th to 34th Street · The grid starts bending below 14th

neighborhoods.name: Chelsea
neighborhoods.fact: The High Line — a 1.45-mile elevated park built on a decommissioned 1930s freight rail line — runs through here. Chelsea piers once docked the Titanic's sister ships.

neighborhoods.name: Gramercy
neighborhoods.fact: The only private park in NYC — residents need an actual iron key to enter. The Flatiron Building at 23rd & Broadway is one of NYC's first skyscrapers (built 1902).

neighborhoods.name: Greenwich Village
neighborhoods.fact: Bob Dylan, Joan Baez, and Jimi Hendrix all lived within blocks of each other here in the 1960s. Stonewall Inn (gay rights movement birthplace) is on Christopher St.

zones.name: Lower Manhattan
zones.street_range: Below 14th Street · Streets have names, not numbers

neighborhoods.name: SoHo
neighborhoods.fact: "South of Houston" — Manhattanites pronounce it HOW-ston (not HEW-ston). The cast-iron commercial buildings from the 1800s are some of the finest in the world.

neighborhoods.name: Tribeca
neighborhoods.fact: "Triangle Below Canal." Robert De Niro co-founded the Tribeca Film Festival here. One of the priciest zip codes in the U.S. — celebrity residents everywhere.

neighborhoods.name: Financial District
neighborhoods.fact: Wall Street's "wall" was a real wooden stockade built in 1653. The NYSE is the world's largest stock exchange. The 9/11 Memorial pools sit where the Twin Towers stood.
